book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
Excursus on the Two Letters of Gregory II. To the Emperor Leo.
title: Excursus on the Two Letters of Gregory II. To the Emperor Leo.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: It is incorrect to say that “the two epistles of Gregory II. have been preserved in the Acts of the Nicene Council” [as Gibbon does].

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: In modern collections of the Acts of Ecclesiastical Councils, they have been printed at the end of the Acts of the Second Nicene Council.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: But they first came to light at the end of the XVIth. century and were printed for the first time in the Annales Ecclesiastici of Baronius, who had obtained them from Fronton le Duc.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: This scholar had copied the text from a Greek ms. at Rheims.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Since then other mss. have been found, the earliest belonging to the XIth., if not the Xth century.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: In another case we should say that the external evidence for the genuineness of the epistles was good.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: We know on the authority of Theophanes that Gregory wrote one or more letters to Leo (ἐπιστολὴν δογματικήν , sub a. m.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: 6221); and we should have no external reasons to suspect copies dating from about 300 years later.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: But the omission of these letters in the Acts of the Nicene Council, though they are stated to have been read at the council, introduces a shadow of suspicion.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: If they were preserved, how comes it that they were not preserved in the Acts of the Council, like the letter of Gregory to the Patriarch Germanus?

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: There is no trace anywhere of the Latin originals.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Turning to the contents, we find enough to convert suspicion into a practical certainty that the documents are forgeries.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: A false date (the beginning of Leo’s reign is placed in the XIVth. instead of the XVth. indiction), and the false implication that the Imperial territory of the “Ducatus Romæ” terminated at twenty-four stadia, or three miles, from Rome, point to an author who was neither a contemporary of Leo nor a resident in Rome.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: But the insolent tone of the letters is enough to condemn them.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Gregory II. would never have addressed to his sovereign the crude abuse with which these documents teem.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Another objection (which I have never seen noticed) is that in the First Letter the famous image of Christ which was pulled down by Leo, is stated to have been in the “Chalkoprateia” (bronzesmith’s quarter), whereas, according to the trustworthy sources, it was above the Chalkâ gate of the Palace.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Rejecting the letters on these grounds—which are supported by a number of smaller points—we get rid of the difficulty about a Lombard siege of Ravenna before a.d.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: 727: a siege which is not mentioned elsewhere and was doubtless created by the confused knowledge of the fabricator.
